section.title: Clean and Unclean, and the Syrophoenician Woman
section.sectionRationale: Jesus confronts the Pharisees over their tradition, exposing how human rules had nullified God's command and locating true defilement in the heart, not in unwashed hands or foods. Mark then shows the gospel crossing the boundary: a Gentile woman's persistent faith wins her daughter's deliverance, and a deaf and mute man in Gentile territory is healed. What defiles and what cleanses are redefined, and the door opens to the nations.
section.thesis: Defilement is not a matter of hands or foods but of the sinful heart, so Jesus declares all foods clean and overturns tradition that voids God's word; and the same Lord, freeing a Gentile girl and opening deaf ears, shows that His cleansing mercy reaches beyond Israel to the nations.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the clash over the tradition of the elders; the teaching on what truly defiles; and two mercies in Gentile lands, the Syrophoenician woman and the deaf-mute man.

units.label: The tradition of the elders
units.note: you nullify the word of God by your tradition

verses.text: Then the Pharisees and some of the scribes who had come from Jerusalem gathered around Jesus,
and they saw some of His disciples eating with hands that were defiled, that is, unwashed.
Now in holding to the tradition of the elders, the Pharisees and all the Jews do not eat until they wash their hands ceremonially.
And on returning from the market, they do not eat unless they wash. And there are many other traditions for them to observe, including the washing of cups, pitchers, kettles, and couches for dining.
So the Pharisees and scribes questioned Jesus: “Why do Your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the elders? Instead, they eat with defiled hands.”
Jesus answered them, “Isaiah prophesied correctly about you hypocrites, as it is written: ‘These people honor Me with their lips, but their hearts are far from Me.
They worship Me in vain; they teach as doctrine the precepts of men.’
You have disregarded the commandment of God to keep the {{paradosis|tradition}} of men.”
He went on to say, “You neatly set aside the command of God to maintain your own tradition.
For Moses said, ‘Honor your father and your mother’ and ‘Anyone who curses his father or mother must be put to death.’
But you say that if a man says to his father or mother, ‘Whatever you would have received from me is {{korban|Corban}}’ (that is, a gift devoted to God),
he is no longer permitted to do anything for his father or mother.
Thus you nullify the word of God by the tradition you have handed down. And you do so in many such matters.”

units.label: What truly defiles
units.note: from within, out of the heart

verses.text: Once again Jesus called the crowd to Him and said, “All of you, listen to Me and understand:
Nothing that enters a man from the outside can {{koinoo|defile}} him; but the things that come out of a man, these are what defile him.”
After Jesus had left the crowd and gone into the house, His disciples inquired about the parable.
“Are you still so dull?” He asked. “Do you not understand? Nothing that enters a man from the outside can defile him,
because it does not enter his heart, but it goes into the stomach and then is eliminated.” (Thus all foods are clean.)
He continued: “What comes out of a man, that is what defiles him.
For from within the hearts of men come evil thoughts, sexual immorality, theft, murder, adultery,
greed, wickedness, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, arrogance, and foolishness.
All these evils come from within, and these are what defile a man.”

units.label: The faith of the Syrophoenician woman
units.note: even the dogs eat the children's crumbs

verses.text: Jesus left that place and went to the region of Tyre. Not wanting anyone to know He was there, He entered a house, but was unable to escape their notice.
Instead, a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit soon heard about Jesus, and she came and fell at His feet.
Now she was a Greek woman of Syrophoenician origin, and she kept asking Jesus to drive the demon out of her daughter.
“First let the children have their fill,” He said. “For it is not right to take the children’s bread and toss it to the dogs.”
“Yes, Lord,” she replied, “even the dogs under the table eat the children’s crumbs.”
Then Jesus told her, “Because of this answer, you may go. The demon has left your daughter.”
And she went home and found her child lying on the bed, and the demon was gone.
Then Jesus left the region of Tyre and went through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee and into the region of the Decapolis.
Some people brought to Him a man who was deaf and hardly able to speak, and they begged Jesus to place His hand on him.
So Jesus took him aside privately, away from the crowd, and put His fingers into the man’s ears. Then He spit and touched the man’s tongue.
And looking up to heaven, He sighed deeply and said to him, “{{ephphatha|Ephphatha}}!” (which means, “Be opened!”).
Immediately the man’s ears were opened and his tongue was released, and he began to speak plainly.
Jesus ordered them not to tell anyone. But the more He ordered them, the more widely they proclaimed it.
The people were utterly astonished and said, “He has done all things well! He makes even the deaf hear and the mute speak!”

paradosis.gloss: A tradition, something handed down. The tradition of the elders was the growing body of human rules meant to guard the law. Jesus condemns not tradition as such but tradition that displaces and even nullifies the commandment of God, honouring human precepts over the divine word.

korban.gloss: Corban, an offering devoted to God. By vowing property as Corban, a person could technically withhold support from aging parents while appearing pious. Jesus exposes this as a way of using religious ritual to evade the plain command to honour father and mother.

koinoo.gloss: To make common or unclean, to defile. Jesus overturns the assumption that defilement comes from without, from foods or unwashed hands. What truly defiles a person is what comes out of the heart; sin, not diet, makes one unclean before God.

ephphatha.gloss: Be opened, the Aramaic word Jesus spoke to the deaf man, preserved by Mark and translated for his readers. The command that opens deaf ears and looses a bound tongue enacts the prophetic hope of Isaiah 35, a sign that the day of salvation has dawned.

basic.context: The Pharisees criticize Jesus' disciples for eating without the ceremonial handwashing their tradition required. Jesus turns the charge back on them: they honour God with their lips while their hearts are far from Him, and their traditions actually cancel God's commands, as when a man declares his money Corban, dedicated to God, and so refuses to help his own parents. Then Jesus teaches that food does not defile a person; defilement comes from within, from the sinful heart. Mark adds two scenes in Gentile territory: a Syrophoenician woman whose witty, humble faith wins her daughter's deliverance, and a deaf man with a speech impediment whom Jesus heals with the word Ephphatha, be opened.
basic.hermeneutics: The theme is what makes a person clean or unclean before God. Jesus relocates the problem from the outside to the heart, and Mark notes that this declares all foods clean, a landmark for the gospel's later welcome of Gentiles. The two Gentile miracles that follow are not incidental; they show the cleansing mercy of Jesus reaching the nations, precisely those the food laws had kept at a distance.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus attacks the paradosis, tradition, that nullifies God's command, exposing the abuse of korban, Corban, a gift devoted to God used to dodge caring for parents. Nothing outside can koinoo, defile, a person; sin from the heart does. And to the deaf man He says ephphatha, be opened.
basic.typology: The opening of deaf ears and a bound tongue fulfils Isaiah 35, the sign of the coming age of salvation. The declaration that all foods are clean anticipates the tearing down of the wall between Jew and Gentile accomplished in Christ. The heart that defiles points to the need for a new heart God promised.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Lord who reads the heart, who has authority to declare all foods clean, and who fulfils the prophecies of the age of salvation by opening ears and loosing tongues. He extends His cleansing mercy to a Gentile woman and a Gentile man.
basic.god: God desires hearts, not mere ritual, and He is grieved when human tradition buries His word. God's mercy is not confined to Israel; He hears the cry of a Gentile mother and opens the ears of a Gentile man.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 29 is quoted against empty worship. Isaiah 35 promises deaf ears opened. Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36 promise a new heart, the answer to the heart that defiles.
basic.oneStory: The problem the whole Bible traces is the human heart, and here Jesus names it: defilement flows from within. He redefines clean and unclean, opens the way to the nations, and enacts the promised age of salvation by opening deaf ears. The story is moving toward the cross, where the unclean heart is cleansed and the wall between Jew and Gentile comes down.

context.literary: Chapter 7 pivots the Gospel outward. The dispute over defilement, verses 1 to 23, sets up the two Gentile miracles, verses 24 to 37, so that the teaching that all foods are clean is immediately illustrated by mercy reaching the ritually excluded nations. Mark's parenthetical comment, thus all foods clean, guides the reader to the theological point.
context.historical: The tradition of the elders was the oral expansion of the law, later codified in the Mishnah, governing ritual washing and much else. Corban vows dedicated property to the temple; some used them to shield assets from family obligations. Tyre, Sidon, and the Decapolis were largely Gentile regions; a Jewish teacher engaging a Syrophoenician woman there crossed sharp ethnic and religious lines.
context.canonical: Jesus quotes Isaiah 29:13 against worship that is all lips and no heart. The healing of the deaf and mute man deliberately echoes Isaiah 35:5-6, then the ears of the deaf unstopped and the tongue of the mute shouting for joy. The declaration that all foods are clean is confirmed to Peter in Acts 10 and expounded in Romans 14 and Colossians 2, opening full fellowship with Gentiles.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To relocate defilement from external rituals to the sinful heart, to expose tradition that nullifies God's word, and to show, through two Gentile miracles, that Jesus' cleansing mercy reaches beyond Israel, preparing the reader for the gospel's mission to the nations.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Jesus' teaching that defilement comes from the heart and that all foods are clean is prescriptive and abiding. The specific first century washing customs and Corban vows are described as examples of a wider principle: no human tradition may be allowed to void God's command. The Gentile miracles reveal God's heart, not a technique for answered prayer.

debates.question: Why does Jesus first seem to refuse the Syrophoenician woman with the words about dogs?

views.label: A test that draws out and honours her faith
views.case: Jesus states the God-ordained order, to the Jew first, using a household image; His words invite rather than dismiss, and He praises the humble, persistent faith her clever reply reveals, granting her request.

views.label: An enacted lesson on the widening mission
views.case: The scene dramatizes for the disciples that the children's bread will indeed reach the nations; even now the crumbs fall from the table, and the Gentile mother receives the very mercy the food laws had fenced off.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Jesus is not harsh or reluctant but is drawing out and commending a faith that becomes a preview of the Gentile welcome the gospel will bring.

debates.question: Does Jesus abolish the Old Testament food laws?

views.label: He fulfils and transcends them, revealing their deeper point
views.case: By declaring all foods clean, Jesus shows that the laws pointed to a deeper holiness of heart; with His coming, the ceremonial distinctions that separated Israel from the nations have served their purpose and give way.

views.label: He exposes their misuse while affirming the moral law
views.case: Jesus targets a religion of external ritual that ignored the heart and even nullified moral commands like honouring parents; the enduring demand is inward purity, which the food laws could never produce.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the point is the heart: outward observance was never the true measure of cleanness, and in Christ the barrier the food laws erected between Jew and Gentile is coming down.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus condemns the paradosis, the tradition handed down, when it displaces and even nullifies God's command, illustrated by korban, Corban, a vow dedicating property to God that some abused to escape supporting their parents. He then overturns the whole assumption that defilement comes from outside: nothing entering a person can koinoo, defile, them, for uncleanness rises from the sinful heart. Mark's aside, thus all foods clean, marks a turning point for the gospel. Finally, to the deaf man Jesus speaks the Aramaic ephphatha, be opened, and ears and tongue obey, enacting the prophetic sign of Isaiah 35 that the age of salvation has come.

typology.type: Deaf ears opened and bound tongues loosed
typology.fulfillment: Jesus enacting the promised age of salvation, then the deaf hear and the mute sing, Isaiah 35:5-6.
The removal of the barrier between Jew and Gentile, confirmed to Peter and fulfilled in the church, Acts 10:9-16 and Ephesians 2:14-16.
The need for the new heart God promised, given through the Spirit under the new covenant, Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 36:26.

study.christ: Jesus stands as the Lord who sees past the outside to the heart, the true interpreter of God's law who distinguishes the divine command from human tradition, and the one with authority to declare all foods clean. He fulfils the prophets' picture of the age of salvation, opening deaf ears and loosing a bound tongue so that the crowds cry, He has done all things well. And He carries His cleansing mercy across the boundary into Gentile lands, hearing a Syrophoenician mother's faith and healing a deaf man in the Decapolis, foreshadowing the gospel that will gather the nations. All of this points to the cross, where the unclean heart is finally cleansed and the dividing wall is torn down.
study.god: God has always desired hearts over ritual, and here Jesus reveals how far a religion of mere externals can drift, even to the point of using devotion to God as a cover for disobeying Him. God is not fooled by lips that honour Him while the heart is far away. And God's mercy is shown to be wider than Israel: He hears the cry of a Gentile mother and opens the ears of a Gentile man, proving that the cleansing He offers is for all who come to Him in faith, of every nation.

crossReferences.note: These people honor Me with their lips, but their hearts are far from Me, quoted by Jesus against empty, tradition-bound worship.
The ears of the deaf unstopped and the tongue of the mute singing, the prophecy enacted in the healing of the deaf-mute man.
God's promise of a new heart and a new spirit, the answer to the heart from which defilement flows.
God tells Peter not to call clean what he called unclean, confirming Jesus' declaration that all foods are clean and opening the door to the Gentiles.

study.oneStory: The Bible traces every evil to the human heart, and here Jesus names it plainly: defilement comes from within. In one movement He redefines clean and unclean, exposes tradition that buries God's word, and then carries His cleansing mercy across the border to a Gentile woman and a Gentile man, enacting the promised age of salvation as deaf ears open. The wall the food laws had built is beginning to fall, and the story presses on toward the cross, where the defiled heart is cleansed and people from every nation are welcomed to the table of God.

quiz.q: What was Jesus' main charge against the Pharisees' tradition?

options.text: They elevated human tradition so highly that it nullified the commandment of God.
options.note: Mark 7:8-13; their tradition, like the Corban abuse, set aside God's clear commands.

options.text: They added extra washings that were merely unnecessary.
options.note: The washings were part of it, but the deeper charge was that tradition voided God's word.

options.text: They failed to keep their own traditions carefully enough.
options.note: The problem was not laxity toward tradition but exalting tradition over God's command.

options.text: They followed the written law of Moses too strictly.
options.note: Jesus upheld Moses; He condemned human tradition that overrode Moses, as with honouring parents.

quiz.q: According to Jesus, what truly defiles a person?

options.text: The evil that comes out of the heart, such as sinful thoughts and deeds, not what enters from outside.
options.note: Mark 7:20-23 locates defilement in the heart, listing sins that flow from within.

options.text: Eating certain foods or failing to wash properly.
options.note: The opposite of Jesus' point; He says food does not defile and declares all foods clean.

options.text: Both unclean food and an unclean heart together.
options.note: Jesus specifically denies that food defiles; He locates defilement in the heart alone.

options.text: Contact with Gentiles and sinners.
options.note: Jesus reaches Gentiles with mercy; defilement is from the heart, not from such contact.

quiz.q: What does Mark's comment, thus all foods are clean, signal?

options.text: A turning point removing the food-law barrier, opening the way to full fellowship with Gentiles.
options.note: The declaration prepares for Acts 10 and the gospel's welcome of the nations.

options.text: That believers may now eat whatever they like.
options.note: True practically, but the deeper significance is the falling of the barrier between Jew and Gentile.

options.text: That the Old Testament food laws were always pointless.
options.note: The laws had a real purpose that pointed beyond themselves; they were not pointless but are now fulfilled.

options.text: That the moral law no longer applies either.
options.note: Jesus intensifies the moral demand of the heart; only the ceremonial food distinction is set aside.

quiz.q: How did the Syrophoenician woman respond to Jesus' words about the children's bread?

options.text: With humble, persistent faith, saying even the dogs eat the children's crumbs, and Jesus granted her request.
options.note: Mark 7:28-29; her faith-filled reply won her daughter's deliverance.

options.text: She accepted His words and quietly went away without her request.
options.note: She did not give up; she pressed her case with faith and received the miracle.

options.text: She persisted in asking and trusted Jesus despite the hard-sounding words.
options.note: True that she persisted, but the fuller answer names her humble, witty faith and the granted request.

options.text: She argued that she deserved the same status as Israel.
options.note: She claimed no equal status; she humbly asked only for crumbs of mercy.

quiz.q: What does the healing of the deaf and mute man reveal?

options.text: That Jesus fulfils the prophetic promise of the age of salvation, opening deaf ears and loosing bound tongues.
options.note: The miracle echoes Isaiah 35:5-6; the crowd marvels that He does all things well.

options.text: That Jesus could heal physical ailments.
options.note: True, but the deeper significance is the fulfilment of Isaiah's sign of the coming salvation.

options.text: That Jesus preferred to heal in private.
options.note: He did take the man aside, but the point is the prophetic sign fulfilled, not a preference for privacy.

options.text: That the man's deafness was caused by his sin.
options.note: The text makes no such claim; the healing displays the dawning age of salvation.

questions: Jesus warned against honouring God with the lips while the heart is far from Him. Where might your worship be more outward form than inward reality?
The Corban rule used religion to dodge a plain duty. Are there ways you use religious activity to avoid the harder obedience God actually asks?
Jesus says defilement comes from within. Which of the sins He lists most needs to be confessed and cleansed in your own heart?
The Syrophoenician woman would not be put off and trusted Jesus for mercy she knew she did not deserve. How does her humble persistence challenge and encourage your own prayers?
The crowds said Jesus does all things well. Where do you need to trust that He is doing all things well, even now, in your life?

translationNotes.note: The BSB does not print a verse 16 in the main text, since the words some manuscripts add here, if anyone has ears to hear, let him hear, are not in the earliest witnesses; the numbering therefore skips from 15 to 17.
The parenthesis thus all foods are clean is Mark's own inspired comment on Jesus' teaching, signalling that the food laws no longer separate; it is confirmed in Acts 10 and Romans 14.
Ephphatha is Aramaic, the everyday language Jesus spoke; Mark preserves the actual word and translates it, be opened, as the deaf ears and bound tongue are freed.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 7:1-37. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when assembling verse text. Verse 16 is absent from the BSB main text and is noted in translationNotes; verses run 1 to 15 then 17 to 37. The em dash in verse 2 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
